# Rules 2026: The Water Transforming the Costa Tropical Between Agricultural Profitability and Real Estate Pressure

The pipeline network of the Béznar-Rules system, a strategic project for the Costa Tropical of Granada, is beginning to materialize its economic impact on the region. With section 9 of the infrastructure in its final phase and over 87% complete, a guaranteed water supply is drawing near for 722 hectares belonging to the *Nuestra Señora Virgen del Rosario* and *Santa Ana* irrigation communities. This first phase, scheduled for completion in July 2026, is key for a booming subtropical sector led by avocados and mangoes, whose high profitability depends directly on water availability. A feasibility report by the state-owned company Acuaes details one of the most anticipated benefits: a drastic reduction in energy pumping costs, estimating savings of over 1.4 million kWh per year for the directly benefited communities—a crucial relief for the competitiveness of their farms.

## The end of thirst: the engineering that changes the landscape
Water has ceased to be a pipe dream to become a flow measured by steel and concrete pipes. In the Guadalfeo valley, the metallic sound of opening valves marks a turning point. We no longer depend on the whim of the well, but on technical management that ensures the survival of mangoes and avocados. This technical precision has changed the face of our fields; where once there was agonizing dry land, today there unfolds an intense, almost surgical green that defies the summer aridity typical of our Granadan land.

## The profitability of 'green gold' versus the calculator
The export of subtropical crops is no longer a gamble; it has consolidated itself as the economic engine of the coast. The 18% increase in the value of foreign sales is not just a number; it is a validation of the efforts of farmers who have known how to read the European market. However, success carries a trap: the value of land with guaranteed water rises so much that, for many, the earth begins to be more profitable as a speculative asset than as a unit of production. It is the farmer's paradox: the better their harvest, the more coveted their soil becomes by those who do not seek to farm, but to build.

## The 400-meter contour line and the expansion of the agricultural frontier
Rising towards the 400-meter contour line is the great technical and territorial challenge of 2026. By reaching these new heights, the Costa Tropical not only expands its productive frontier but redefines the boundaries between the mountain and the sea. It is difficult terrain, with steep slopes, where irrigation requires a mastery few possess. Here, the family farm model faces the challenge of scale; without precise organization, expansion into the heights could fragment an ecosystem that until now had remained compact, forcing municipalities to rethink what they want to become when water reaches its highest levels.

## The shadow of brickwork over the cultivated terrace
Real estate pressure on the Granadan coast is not new, but its nature has changed. It is no longer just the beachfront that is sought; now, foreign investors look for the "countryside experience" with views of the Mediterranean. This eagerness to convert rustic land into exclusive residences creates a muffled but constant tension. While administrations celebrate the arrival of water as an agricultural triumph, deals are being closed in notary offices that, in the medium term, may leave the new pipelines feeding lawns instead of productive orchards. Maintaining the balance is the titanic task of this decade.

## A future carved with rigor and caution
The Costa Tropical must not become a mere luxury backdrop for seasonal tourism. We have the water, we have the climate, and we have the know-how of generations who have mastered the art of farming under the southern sun. What we lack is the determination to protect productive soil from an urban vortex that, in the long run, would leave us with luxury pipes and without the essence of our land. Observing these sections of Rules is not just looking at a public work; it is observing the roadmap for our own economic survival. We must be precise, we must be demanding, and, above all, we must know that the land, once it is concreted over, will never bear fruit again.

Until next time — the Coast awaits you, from Almuñécar to the Axarquía.
